title: Vignette: Using the Count Regression Toolkit
text: This vignette demonstrates how to use the Shiny App to fit and evaluate count regression models.

title: Vignette: Using the Count Regression Toolkit
text: This vignette demonstrates how to use the Shiny App to fit and evaluate count regression models.

title: Vignette: Using the Count Regression Toolkit
text:
Step 1: Load Data
Use the sample dataset:
Ache Monkey (McMillan)
Response variable:

Kills (number of successful hunts)

Predictor:

Age

title: Vignette: Using the Count Regression Toolkit
section: Step 2: Look at summary of data
text:
Step 2: Look at summary of data
After choosing “Poisson” as our model type and pressing Fit Model, the “Data Summary” tab should show:

Mean: 2.787
Variance: 40.171

Clearly, the variance is much larger than the mean, which indicates overdispersion.

title: Vignette: Using the Count Regression Toolkit
text:
Step 3: Check conditions and diagnostics
The “Model Conditions” and “Diagnostics” tabs show a dispersion ratio of 11.679, confirming the overdispersion, and indicating we should try an alternative model.

title: Vignette: Using the Count Regression Toolkit
text:
Step 4: Try alternative
Try Negative Binomial so that we can use AIC and log-likelihood, while hopefully fixing the overdispersion issue. So fit the model again but as a Negative Binomial Regression.

title: Vignette: Using the Count Regression Toolkit
text:
Step 5: New diagnostics
“Diagnostics” tab now shows an improved RQR plot and Pearson residual plot, indicating a better fit has been found. This can be confirmed with the “Model Comparison” tab, by comparing the AIC’s. A Generalized Poisson model also looks appropriate, so that can be used as well.

title: Vignette: Using the Count Regression Toolkit
text:
Step 6: Interpretation
From Model Output for Negative Binomial:
Holding other variables constant, a one-unit increase in Age is associated with a 5.73% increase in the expected count of kills.
From Model Output for Generalized Poisson:
Holding other variables constant, a one-unit increase in Age is associated with a 3.27% increase in the expected count of kills.
This interpretation is based on the exponentiated predictor coefficient.

title: About
text:
This project is a statistical software toolkit designed to automate count regression analysis using an interactive Shiny dashboard. The count regression models include:

Poisson Regression
Negative Binomial Regression
Quasi-Poisson Regression
Zero Inflated Model
Generalized Poisson Regression

title: About
text:
This project is a statistical software toolkit designed to automate count regression analysis using an interactive Shiny dashboard. The count regression models include:

Poisson Regression
Negative Binomial Regression
Quasi-Poisson Regression
Zero Inflated Model
Generalized Poisson Regression

title: About
text:
Applications
Count regression can be used on data such as number of events (natural disasters, disease cases, etc), number of occurrences of something over time, or it can be used for frequency data.
The toolkit allows users to:

Upload data
Specify regression models
Model fitting and condition checks
Model comparisons
Analyze and interpret model summary and ouput

title: About
text:
How to Use the App

Upload your own dataset or choose a sample dataset
Specify a model formula (e.g., Kills ~ Age + Days)
Select a model type
Click Fit Model
Click through tabs to evaluate and interpret your results

title: About
text:
Author
Danny Molyneux

title: Count Regression Toolkit

title: Count Regression Toolkit
text: This website documents my Data Analysis II software toolkit for count regression.

title: Count Regression Toolkit
text:
Launching the App
The Shiny app is included in this GitHub repository: https://github.com/dannymolyneux/dannymolyneux.github.io
To run the app:

Clone the repository from GitHub:
https://github.com/dannymolyneux/dannymolyneux.github.io.git
Open R or RStudio
Run:

shiny::runApp()
Or open the “app.r” file, and click Run App.

title: Count Regression Toolkit

title: AI Appendix
text: This appendix documents how/where AI tools were used to assist me with this project.

title: AI Appendix
text: This appendix documents how/where AI tools were used to assist me with this project.

title: AI Appendix
text:
Datasets
Helped create and format example “bad” dataset to test missing values

title: AI Appendix
text:
Condition Checks and Diagnostics

Assisted in formatting the condition table

Suggested the use of a helper function:

yn <- function(x) ifelse(isTRUE(x), "Yes", "No")

Helped with the html formatting for interpreting dispersion ratio and zero-inflation diagnostics

title: AI Appendix
text:
Model Comparison

Helped implement tryCatch() syntax when running model comparison across all candidate models

Assisted with html formatting for recommended model text

title: AI Appendix
text:
Model Output and Interpretation

Provided help with formatting interpretation text for coefficients

Gave guidance for separating interpretations in zero-inflated models

Helped with some of the ggplot2 syntax for the estimated marginal means plots

title: AI Appendix
text:
User Interface

Assisted with HTML formatting
